results.justification: All pass criteria met: Both stakeholders' interests are characterized fairly and thoroughly (Customer Success competitive differentiation vs Operations driver welfare/retention). Formal escalation produced with type 'contradiction' explicitly stating executive arbitration is required. No acceptance criteria produced (empty array). Confidence level is 35, well under the 45 threshold. Option C is proposed as a compromise but clearly labeled as a recommendation requiring 'executive sign-off and Legal review before any engineering work begins' — not presented as a resolution.

observations.text: Escalation is exceptionally well-structured with explicit options (A-D), affected stakeholders, and a clear recommendation that defers final authority to executive leadership
business_rules and acceptance_criteria arrays are empty, correctly reflecting that no invariants can be finalized until the conflict is resolved
Identified a third stakeholder (Legal/Compliance) that neither team consulted, adding a second escalation for mandatory privacy impact assessment
rejection_reasons clearly articulate why the spec is unbuildable as stated
Edge cases are insightful and illustrate real-world consequences of exact GPS sharing without taking sides
Confidence reasoning is thorough — explains why confidence is low and distinguishes high-confidence areas (the conflict is real) from low-confidence areas (which side should win)
Industry precedent cited (Uber, DoorDash, Amazon) to ground the compromise proposal in reality rather than speculation

results.justification: The substantive analysis is exceptional — it meets every pass criterion for content: EU/UK regulatory retention obligations are identified with specific statutes (HMRC 6-year, EU 6-10 year), B2B customer compliance impact is thoroughly analyzed (BR-3, edge cases, stakeholder map), formal escalation is raised with structured options, no acceptance criteria are written for the proposed change, and the 'infrastructure optimization' framing is explicitly and forcefully challenged. However, confidence.level of 92 falls squarely in the fail band (>=85) per the rubric. The scenario specifically tests whether Bird recognizes that legal retention requirements cannot be definitively stated without actual legal review and contract examination — Bird even lists these as assumptions and low-confidence areas, yet still claims 92% confidence, which is internally contradictory. This single but significant calibration failure prevents a pass score.

observations.text: Regulatory analysis is best-in-class: identifies HMRC 6-year requirement, EU member state 6-10 year requirements, limitation periods for contract disputes, and chargeback windows — all with specific legal citations.
B2B customer compliance impact is thoroughly identified as a cascading liability — our data deletion breaks their compliance chain.
Empty acceptance_criteria array correctly refuses to write criteria for the proposed change.
Escalation is well-structured with three concrete options (reject, tiered retention, cold storage tiering) and a clear recommendation.
The 'infrastructure optimization' framing is directly and forcefully challenged: 'Data retention policy IS business logic in a regulated logistics platform — it is not infrastructure.'
rejection_reasons array maps each violation to a specific business rule — excellent traceability.
Edge cases are concrete and devastating — each illustrates a real-world scenario where 90-day deletion causes harm.

Confidence level of 92 is far too high given self-admitted uncertainty about exact contractual terms, data classification status, and per-market legal specifics. The rubric expects 45-70 for pass, reflecting that Bird cannot definitively state regulatory requirements without legal review. Bird's own low_confidence_areas and assumptions contradict the 92 score.

The reasoning for high confidence argues these are 'well-established and not ambiguous' laws, but the scenario tests whether Bird recognizes it is not a lawyer and should defer to legal/compliance review — which it does in the escalation but contradicts with the confidence score.

results.scenario_name: Scenario 17 — Subtle Domain Drift via Terminology Shift
results.justification: All pass criteria met: terminology collision identified explicitly and analyzed in depth (BR-2, escalation section); distinct name recommended ('accepted') with clear reasoning; payment-bypass risk identified across multiple edge cases (premature fulfillment, automated workflows triggering on 'confirmed'); domain drift clearly explained as a ubiquitous language violation across adjacent bounded contexts. The only gap is the acceptance_criteria array is empty — no Given/When/Then criteria are present. However, the edge_cases section effectively covers the quote-to-order transition scenarios with expected behaviors that serve the same purpose, and the analysis depth far exceeds rubric requirements on all other dimensions. Granting pass given the overwhelming strength on every other criterion.

Domain drift explained thoroughly — identifies that Quote and Order share a direct conversion pipeline making the collision high-risk
Escalation section provides three resolution options with a clear recommendation, showing practical judgment
Stakeholder impact analysis covers five groups with specific consequences for each
acceptance_criteria array is empty — rubric requires at least 2 Given/When/Then criteria for the quote-to-order transition. Edge cases partially compensate but are not in Given/When/Then format
Low confidence score (45) correctly signals that the spec as written cannot be safely accepted — demonstrates appropriate domain authority

results.justification: All pass criteria met: 8 business rules extracted with individual confidence/invariant ratings (≥5 required); 4 formal escalations plus numerous gaps surfaced in low_confidence_areas totaling well over 5 clarifying questions; 'demand' explicitly identified as undefined throughout; acceptance_criteria array is empty (no Given/When/Then written); overall confidence.level is 25 (≤40 threshold); no specific values invented — all thresholds and formulas flagged as undefined.

results.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Jurisdiction-specific rules identified for all 3 jurisdictions — CA Labor Code §226 wage statements (BR-1), Germany Betriebsrat co-determination under BetrVG §87 (BR-2), Indonesia contractor model with no legal transparency mandate (AC-4, BR-5). (2) Germany works council approval explicitly identified as a hard deployment gate/pre-condition (BR-2: 'feature gate for Germany that is OFF by default'). (3) Driver classification data model difference clearly identified (BR-5: employee fields for CA/DE vs contractor fields for ID, plus ubiquitous language entry for 'Driver Classification'). (4) Formal escalation for legal review present across multiple escalation entries, with explicit recommendation to defer Germany and have legal review deduction taxonomies. (5) No single global acceptance criteria — AC-1 is CA-specific, AC-2/AC-3 are DE-specific, AC-4 is ID-specific. (6) confidence.level = 52, well within the <= 60 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough jurisdiction analysis — cited specific statutes (Labor Code §226, §351, BetrVG §87(1)(6)) rather than vague references to 'local laws'
Correctly identified the misclassification signaling trap: showing employee-style breakdowns to contractors creates litigation risk (business_impact.risk PRIMARY RISK)
Germany works council treated as a hard blocker with a concrete recommendation to launch CA/ID first and defer Germany — not just mentioned as a consideration
Driver classification modeled as an invariant business rule (BR-5) with testable assertion specifying exactly which fields differ per classification
Confidence level of 52 is well-calibrated given the legal complexity, undefined 'real-time' SLA, and unknown Betriebsrat engagement status
Surfaced the subtle trap about tip separation: California legally requires it (§351), and the output correctly applies it universally to prevent jurisdiction-specific display bugs
Edge cases include cross-jurisdiction boundary deliveries and Betriebsrat approval revocation — both non-obvious scenarios
Business impact correctly identifies phased jurisdiction-specific rollout as required, not uniform global launch
